failure_modes.text: The hammer problem appears when teams force a familiar specialty frame onto novel work.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test frame fit before applying the specialty lens: list what the familiar framework explains and what it leaves out, and switch tools when the gap is material.

failure_modes.text: Overconfidence grows when experts assume deep knowledge in one lane is enough for the whole problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Pair deep expertise with structured cross-domain challenge: require specialists to present their analysis to someone from an adjacent discipline who can surface blind spots.

failure_modes.text: Focus can become identity lock-in that resists adjacent skills or broader synthesis when the situation changes.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build identity flexibility: encourage experts to define themselves by the problems they solve, not the tools they use, so they can adopt new methods when the situation demands it.

premortem_questions.text: Are we going deeper on the right narrow problem, or just protecting an old identity?
What different frame from another discipline would expose what our specialty misses?
What would we have to believe for this specialized scope to remain the highest-leverage lane?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.text: Keep the single focus principle visible and remove work that does not support it.
Do not boil the ocean; gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the key thesis.
Define the specialty as one specific problem for one specific audience in one specific way.
heuristics.source_quote: 1 Specific Problem, for 1 Specific Type of Person, in 1 Specific Way
